title: Patterns from the canon
subtitle: Vol. 1 — community handbook
intro: How a chatbot-user thinks about the system. The interface is the chat thread; the iteration pipeline is the community of chatbot-users filing patches via the wiki. An internal user-sim agent (Claude Code role) drafted these diagrams ahead of public launch by dogfooding the same patch + ship channels real users will use.

diagrams.title: What happens inside a single node
diagrams.caption: Every node in every variant follows the same lifecycle. Understanding this one loop explains the whole system.
diagrams.takeaway: Compute is only spent in WORKING (the rest of the lifecycle is bookkeeping); IDLE is the default state — a team of 20 with only 3 active nodes is burning tokens for 3, not 20.

diagrams.title: Three-node, fully annotated
diagrams.caption: Atomic shape: lead → produce → verify. Solid edges = state writes; dashed = control signals.
diagrams.takeaway: The annotation grammar (state vs control, fan-out vs fan-in) carries from this 3-node up to the 20-node production case.

diagrams.title: Gate routing matrix
diagrams.caption: Gates classify: ACCEPT, route_back, REJECT. Rows are gate decisions; columns are resolution tiers.
diagrams.takeaway: Scaling adds specialization to the failure-class vocabulary. More nodes = finer gate resolution = more precise retries.

diagrams.title: Gate resolution tiers
diagrams.caption: Resolution doubles per tier. Higher resolution = fewer false retries; lower resolution = faster pipelines.
diagrams.takeaway: Pick the resolution your work-class actually needs. Don't over-resolve cheap work; don't under-resolve expensive work.

diagrams.title: Parallel execution zones
diagrams.caption: Fan-out edges spawn concurrent nodes; fan-in edges are barriers. Zones are graph-topology, not an execution mode.
diagrams.takeaway: Parallelism falls out of edge shape. There's no separate scheduler — the DAG is the schedule.

diagrams.title: Twenty-node production team
diagrams.caption: The same lead → produce → verify pattern, scaled. Real teams shipping real work.
diagrams.takeaway: Same atomic shape as diagram 02. Resolution is finer; pattern is the same.

diagrams.title: Execution timeline + iteration
diagrams.caption: Gantt-style trace of a single run: which nodes fired when, which edges carried state, where retries happened.
diagrams.takeaway: The gate is a classifier, not a valve — it emits routing decisions; an outer runner does the actual looping.

diagrams.title: Four variants side-by-side
diagrams.caption: 3 / 5 / 10 / 20-node teams pursuing the same goal at different resolutions.
diagrams.takeaway: Every variant is the same pattern, different resolution. lead → produce → verify → route is the invariant.

diagrams.title: Trigger summary
diagrams.caption: The vertical band of trigger conditions that fire each node. Edges are triggers, not schedules.
diagrams.takeaway: A node fires when its upstream writes its input fields — pure data-availability, no time-based scheduler.

diagrams.title: The through-line
diagrams.caption: If you read the diagrams from top to bottom: nine invariants the whole goals surface inherits.
diagrams.takeaway: Same pattern, different resolution. The lifecycle from diagram 01 is the atom; everything else is composition.
